title: HW2 Extra 1 — $T(n) = T(9n/10) + T(n/10) + n$
statement: Prove $T(n) = O(n \log n)$ by induction, and draw a recursion tree. $T(1) = T(2) = 1$.

steps.title: (a) Inductive upper bound.
steps.body:
**Claim:** $T(n) \le Cn \log_2 n$ for $n \ge 2$.

**Base $n = 2$:** $T(2) = 1 \le C \cdot 2 \log_2 2 = 2C$. So $C \ge 1/2$ suffices.

**I.H.:** For all $2 \le k < n$, $T(k) \le Ck\log_2 k$.

**Step:**
$$T(n) = T(9n/10) + T(n/10) + n \le C\cdot\tfrac{9n}{10}\log_2(9n/10) + C\cdot\tfrac{n}{10}\log_2(n/10) + n.$$

Expand using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:

- $\tfrac{9n}{10}\log_2(9n/10) = \tfrac{9n}{10}(\log_2 n + \log_2(9/10))$.
- $\tfrac{n}{10}\log_2(n/10) = \tfrac{n}{10}(\log_2 n + \log_2(1/10))$.

Add them: coefficient of $\log_2 n$ is $\tfrac{9n}{10} + \tfrac{n}{10} = n$. So $Cn\log_2 n$ appears. The leftover:
$$Cn\left(\tfrac{9}{10}\log_2\tfrac{9}{10} + \tfrac{1}{10}\log_2\tfrac{1}{10}\right) = Cn \cdot (-0.469\ldots) \approx -0.469 Cn.$$

So $T(n) \le Cn\log_2 n - 0.469 Cn + n$. For $C \ge 1/0.469 \approx 2.14$, the $-0.469 Cn + n \le 0$, giving $T(n) \le Cn\log_2 n$. ✓

tips.label: Entropy-like term
tips.content: The $p \log p + (1-p)\log(1-p)$ term for $p = 9/10$ evaluates to about $-0.469$. This is the negative of a Shannon-entropy expression; whenever the branch fractions sum to 1, you always get a negative leftover, which is the slack that absorbs the $+n$.

steps.title: (b) Recursion tree — level work is constant $n$.
steps.body:
A key observation: at each full level, the subproblem sizes sum to $n$.

- Level 0: one node of size $n$. Level work $= n$.
- Level 1: sizes $9n/10, n/10$. Total size $= n$. Level work $= n$.
- Level 2: sizes $(9/10)^2 n, (9/10)(1/10) n, (1/10)(9/10) n, (1/10)^2 n$. Sum $= n$. Level work $= n$.
- More generally: a node of size $k$ contributes subproblem sizes $9k/10$ and $k/10$, summing to $k$. So level-total-size is preserved.

**So each full level contributes $n$ non-recursive work.**

steps.title: (b) Tree height.
steps.body:
Shortest branch: always follow the $1/10$ child → height $\log_{10} n$.

Longest branch: always follow the $9/10$ child → height $\log_{10/9} n = O(\log n)$.

Both are $O(\log n)$, so the number of full levels is $O(\log n)$. Total work: $O(n \log n)$. Matches the inductive bound.

tips.label: Branches of unequal length
tips.content: Unlike balanced trees (Merge Sort), some branches end earlier. That's fine — the upper bound on total work is the number of levels in the deepest branch times the per-level work.

answerKey: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. Each full level contributes exactly $n$ non-recursive work (subproblem sizes always sum to $n$), and there are $O(\log n)$ levels.
